Vitamin A Supplementation: Saving Children’s Lives Worldwide

Author

Sujal Boodgere Sunil - A00096721

Published

April 15, 2026

1 Vitamin A Supplementation: Saving Children’s Lives Worldwide

1.1 Executive Summary

Vitamin A is an essential micronutrient required for healthy vision, strong immunity, and proper growth in children. Deficiency remains a major yet preventable public health issue, particularly in low- and middle-income countries, contributing to childhood blindness and increased mortality from common infections.

Routine Vitamin A supplementation has been proven to reduce under-five mortality by up to 23% among children aged 6–59 months. This report analyses global coverage trends using UNICEF data, explores its relationship with economic indicators and child mortality (ages 5–9), and provides evidence-based recommendations.

1.2 Why This Issue Matters

Vitamin A deficiency weakens children’s immune systems and can cause irreversible blindness. Despite global progress, millions of children still lack access to adequate nutrition or supplementation. Effective twice-yearly supplementation is one of the most cost-effective interventions to protect child health and support long-term development.

1.3 Key Findings

  • Global average coverage has improved since 2000, but substantial regional disparities persist.
  • Higher GDP per capita shows a strong positive correlation with better supplementation coverage.
  • Several countries continue to report coverage rates below 10%, leaving large populations of children vulnerable.
  • A noticeable decline in coverage occurred in 2020 due to disruptions caused by the COVID-19 pandemic.
  • Low supplementation coverage is frequently associated with higher child mortality rates in the 5–9 age group.

1.4 Data & Methodology

UNICEF Indicator 1 (Vitamin A Supplementation Coverage) and Indicator 2 (Mortality rate age 5–9) were merged with World Bank metadata using the Polars library in Python. Data cleaning, filtering, joining, grouping, and summarisation were performed efficiently. All visualisations were created using the plotnine package for clarity and reproducibility.

1.5 Visual Analysis

1.5.1 World Map – Vitamin A Supplementation Coverage (2023)

This choropleth map highlights strong coverage in East Asia, Latin America, and parts of South Asia, while revealing critical gaps in many sub-Saharan African and conflict-affected countries.

1.5.2 Bar Chart – 10 Countries with the Lowest Coverage in 2023

These countries face the most urgent challenges and require immediate strengthening of supplementation programmes.

1.5.3 Scatterplot with Linear Regression – GDP per Capita vs Coverage

The clear upward regression line demonstrates a strong positive relationship between economic development and successful Vitamin A programme implementation.

1.5.4 Time-Series Chart – Global Average Coverage Trend (2000–2023)

Long-term progress is evident; however, the sharp dip in 2020 underscores the vulnerability of child health interventions to global crises.

1.6 Recommendations & Call to Action

To accelerate progress and protect more children, the following actions are strongly recommended:

  • Strengthen supply chain logistics to ensure reliable and timely delivery of supplements, even in remote areas.
  • Integrate Vitamin A supplementation with routine immunisation and maternal-child health programmes.
  • Implement community-based awareness campaigns to improve parental understanding and participation.
  • Provide targeted technical and financial support to countries with persistently low coverage rates.

Every additional percentage point in coverage has the potential to save thousands of young lives annually.
